Pooled 3-Cohort Neuropsych: Spontaneous Reversal (Class 2 Crude)

Minghui (Grace) Wang

2026-07-10

0.Overview

This notebook finds spontaneous reversal of cognitive aging using Class 2 (follow-up-as-time) models.

Algorithm. We use each participant-test combination that has at least 2 valid visits for that test. We take the observed score change from baseline to the first follow-up and turn it into a per-year (annualized) change, and compare it to the model-estimated change over the 5, 10, 15, and 20-year horizons. Every model change is computed directly from the fitted model at the participant’s exact baseline age (fixed effects only, model.matrix %*% fixef); we do not round the age to a grid.

For each horizon h, let model_change be the direction-adjusted model change, and obs be the direction-adjusted annualized observed change:

  • If model_change < 0 (expected decline): reversal when obs > (-1) * model_change.
  • If model_change > 0 (expected improvement): reversal when obs > 2 * model_change.
  • If model_change = 0 (no expected change): reversal when obs > 0.

For Trails tests (higher = worse), we flip the sign of both the observed change and the model change, so that positive always means improvement.

Outputs.

  • Table 4 (exclusive count): age-bin counts. Each participant-test combination is counted once, in the column for its maximum qualifying horizon.
  • Table 4 (cumulative count): age-bin counts. Each column counts everyone who qualifies at that horizon or higher.
  • Table 4 (cumulative percentage): the cumulative counts divided by N. This is the spontaneous reversal rate in each age bin.
  • Scatter plots: annualized observed change vs baseline age, colored by the maximum reversal horizon. Restricted to baseline age 50 to 90, to match Table 4.

1. Setup

1.1 Output folders

# Use the same 2-visit crude analysis sample as the fitted models.
SAMPLE <- "2visit"
covariates <- NULL
source("R/setup_analysis_sample.R")

model_tag <- if (SAMPLE == "2visit") "crude_2visit" else "crude"
dir_tag   <- if (SAMPLE == "2visit") "_2visit" else ""

class2_fits_file <- "pooled3_crude_2visit_class2_fits.rds"

plot_dir     <- paste0(
  "../output/plots/pooled3_05_spontaneous_reversal_class2_crude", dir_tag
)
table_dir    <- paste0(
  "../output/tables/pooled3_05_spontaneous_reversal_class2_crude", dir_tag
)
dir.create(plot_dir,     recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create(table_dir,    recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

1.2 Load saved Class 2 models

model_rds_dir <- "../output/models"

class2_fits <- readRDS(file.path(model_rds_dir, class2_fits_file))

class2_fits_success <- class2_fits |>
  filter(fit_success)

cat(
  "Loaded", nrow(class2_fits_success),
  "successfully fitted Class 2 model strata\n"
)
## Loaded 28 successfully fitted Class 2 model strata
class2_fits_success |>
  select(test, sex_label, n_obs, n_subjects, subject_random_slope_used) |>
  knitr::kable()

1.3 Test dictionary and score direction

test_dictionary <- make_pooled_test_dictionary(model_ready_pool) # defined in R/workbook_writers.R.
test_dictionary |> knitr::kable()
test test_label xprize_subdomain range
dsf Digit Span Forward Attention 0-9
dsb Digit Span Backward Executive Function & Processing 0-8
trailsb Trail Making Test Part B Executive Function & Processing 0.32-10.00 minutes; maximum time set at 10 minutes
lmd Logical Memory, delayed recall Memory 0-23
lmi Logical Memory, immediate recall Memory 0-23
lmr Logical Memory, recognition Memory 0-11
pasd Paired Associates, delayed recall Memory 0-10
pasi Paired Associates, immediate recall Memory 0-21
pasr Paired Associates, recognition Memory 0-10
coding_correct WAIS-IV Coding, total correct Psychomotor 0-119
trailsa Trail Making Test Part A Psychomotor 0.32-10.00 minutes; maximum time set at 10 minutes
vrd Visual Reproductions, delayed recall Visual Learning 0-14
vri Visual Reproductions, immediate recall Visual Learning 0-14
vrr Visual Reproductions, recognition Visual Learning 0-14

Trails A and Trails B are scored as completion time (higher = worse). For these tests, both observed and model changes are sign-flipped so that positive always means improvement.

# `higher_is_worse_tests` is defined in `R/spontaneous_reversal_functions.R`.

test_dictionary |>
  mutate(
    score_direction = if_else(test %in% higher_is_worse_tests, -1L, 1L),
    direction_note  = if_else(
      score_direction == -1L,
      "Higher is worse (completion time); sign-flipped",
      "Higher is better"
    )
  ) |>
  select(test, test_label, score_direction, direction_note) |>
  knitr::kable()
test test_label score_direction direction_note
dsf Digit Span Forward 1 Higher is better
dsb Digit Span Backward 1 Higher is better
trailsb Trail Making Test Part B -1 Higher is worse (completion time); sign-flipped
lmd Logical Memory, delayed recall 1 Higher is better
lmi Logical Memory, immediate recall 1 Higher is better
lmr Logical Memory, recognition 1 Higher is better
pasd Paired Associates, delayed recall 1 Higher is better
pasi Paired Associates, immediate recall 1 Higher is better
pasr Paired Associates, recognition 1 Higher is better
coding_correct WAIS-IV Coding, total correct 1 Higher is better
trailsa Trail Making Test Part A -1 Higher is worse (completion time); sign-flipped
vrd Visual Reproductions, delayed recall 1 Higher is better
vri Visual Reproductions, immediate recall 1 Higher is better
vrr Visual Reproductions, recognition 1 Higher is better

2. Participant-level reversal classification

2.1. Observed change (baseline to first follow-up)

We use each participant-test combination that has at least 2 valid visits for that test. The annualized change is (score_followup1 - score_baseline) / followup_years. The observed-change table (and the follow-up summary below) keeps only baseline age 50 to 90, to match Table 4 and the scatter plots.

2.1.1 Compute per stratum

# For each successfully fitted test × sex stratum, compute participant-level observed change from baseline to the first follow-up visit, then restrict to index baseline age 50-90.

# Input:
#   data = stratum-specific analysis data stored in class2_fits_success

# Output:
#   obs = a nested participant-level table with one row per participant-test combination (baseline age 50-90), including baseline age, follow-up interval, raw change, and annualized observed change.

class2_with_obs <- class2_fits_success |>
  mutate(
    obs = map(
      data,
      \(data_x) {
        data_x |>
          # defined in R/spontaneous_reversal_functions.R：
          compute_obs_change() |> 
          restrict_reversal_baseline_age()       
          # index baseline age 50-90 only, applied here so subject_obs, summaries, model changes, classification, Table 4, and plots all share the same sample.
      }
    )
  )

# Unnest stratum-specific observed-change tables into one participant-level table used for summaries and downstream reversal classification.
subject_obs <- class2_with_obs |>
  select(test, sex_label, obs) |>
  unnest(obs)

cat(
  "Observed change computed for",
  n_distinct(subject_obs$dbgap_subject_id),
  "unique subjects across",
  n_distinct(subject_obs$test), "tests\n"
)
## Observed change computed for 3495 unique subjects across 14 tests

2.1.2 Follow-up interval summary

subject_obs |>
  group_by(test, sex_label) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    median_fu_years = median(followup_years),
    iqr_lo = quantile(followup_years, 0.25),
    iqr_hi = quantile(followup_years, 0.75),
    min_nonzero_fu_years = if_else(
      any(followup_years > 0, na.rm = TRUE),
      min(followup_years[followup_years > 0], na.rm = TRUE),
      NA_real_
    ),
    max_fu_years = max(followup_years),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1)
test sex_label n median_fu_years iqr_lo iqr_hi min_nonzero_fu_years max_fu_years
coding_correct Women 561 5.2 4.1 6.0 1.0 8.8
coding_correct Men 485 5.2 4.1 5.9 1.0 9.0
dsb Women 1630 5.3 2.4 7.0 0.2 20.3
dsb Men 1231 5.3 2.9 7.0 0.5 17.0

pasd Men 1241 5.9 4.1 6.8 0.5 17.1
pasi Women 1744 5.8 3.3 6.9 0.2 19.0
pasi Men 1382 5.9 3.8 6.8 0.5 17.4
pasr Women 1139 5.9 3.6 7.2 0.3 13.1
pasr Men 898 5.8 4.3 7.2 0.7 12.0
trailsa Women 1567 5.9 3.6 6.8 0.4 19.0
trailsa Men 1301 5.9 4.1 6.8 0.5 18.0
trailsb Women 1526 5.9 3.6 6.9 0.4 19.0
trailsb Men 1275 5.9 4.1 6.8 0.5 18.0
vrd Women 1803 5.9 3.5 6.9 0.2 19.0
vrd Men 1450 5.9 3.9 6.8 0.5 17.1
vri Women 1816 5.9 3.5 6.9 0.2 19.0
vri Men 1460 5.9 3.9 6.8 0.5 17.1
vrr Women 1747 5.8 3.4 6.9 0.2 19.0
vrr Men 1393 5.9 3.9 6.8 0.5 17.1

2.2. Model-estimated changes (Class 2)

We compute the model-derived threshold directly from the fitted model, using fixed effects only (model.matrix %*% fixef). We do not read any value from Table 2.

Class 2 separates baseline age from follow-up time. For a participant indexed at baseline age A and horizon h, the reference start age is A − h. The threshold is the expected h-year aging change from the reference start age A − h up to age A. In Class 2 this is a follow-up change that starts from reference baseline age A − h and runs h years (the reference-start-age contrast):

Pred_Class2(baseline_age = A - h, t_followup = h)
- Pred_Class2(baseline_age = A - h, t_followup = 0)

This uses the same contrast structure as Table 2 for an h-year follow-up change starting from baseline age A − h, but we evaluate it directly from the fitted model at the participant’s exact age. We do not round to, or look up, the displayed 5-year Table 2 grid.

For example, if A = 72, the 5/10/15/20-year thresholds use:

  • baseline_age = 67, t_followup 0 to 5
  • baseline_age = 62, t_followup 0 to 10
  • baseline_age = 57, t_followup 0 to 15
  • baseline_age = 52, t_followup 0 to 20

Because we use the exact age, a 72.4-year-old uses reference start ages 67.4 / 62.4 / 57.4 / 52.4.

## Compute per stratum
class2_with_mc <- class2_with_obs |>
  mutate(
    model_changes = pmap(
      list(model, obs),
      function(model_x, obs_x) {
        # model_x = test × sex fitted Class 2 model
        # obs_x   = test × sex observed-change participant table
        compute_model_change_class2(model_x, unique(obs_x$baseline_age)) # defined in R/spontaneous_reversal_functions.R.
        # unique(obs_x$baseline_age) = participant-specific observed baseline age 
      }
    )
  )

2.3. Reversal classification

Join observed changes with model-estimated changes and apply the spontaneous reversal rule.

2.3.1 Classify

# For each participant-test record, join observed change with exact-age model-estimated changes, then apply the reversal threshold rules.

reversal_subject_level <- class2_with_mc |>
  mutate(
    classified = pmap(
      list(obs, model_changes, test, sex_label),
      function(obs_x, mc_x, test_x, sex_x) {
        obs_x |>                                       # participant-level observed change
          left_join(mc_x, by = "baseline_age") |>      # exact-age model change
          mutate(test = test_x, sex_label = sex_x) |>
          classify_reversal()                          # defined in R/spontaneous_reversal_functions.R
      }
    )
  ) |>
  select(classified) |>
  unnest(classified) |>
  restrict_reversal_baseline_age()

cat(
  "Classified", nrow(reversal_subject_level),
  "participant-test combinations (baseline age 50-90) across",
  n_distinct(reversal_subject_level$test), "tests\n"
)
## Classified 39599 participant-test combinations (baseline age 50-90) across 14 tests

2.3.2 Reversal summary

reversal_subject_level |>
  group_by(test, sex_label) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    n_any_rev = sum(max_reversal_year > 0),
    pct_any = 100 * mean(max_reversal_year > 0),
    n_rev_5  = sum(reversal_5yr),
    n_rev_10 = sum(reversal_10yr),
    n_rev_15 = sum(reversal_15yr),
    n_rev_20 = sum(reversal_20yr),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1)
test sex_label n n_any_rev pct_any n_rev_5 n_rev_10 n_rev_15 n_rev_20

3. Output table 4

The participant-level classifications were summarized by baseline-age group in exclusive, cumulative, and cumulative-percentage formats.

3.1 Table 4 (exclusive form)

table4_exclusive <- make_table4(
  reversal_subject_level, test_dictionary, form = "exclusive"
)

# Preview one test.
table4_exclusive |>
  filter(test == "lmd") |>
  select(-xprize_subdomain, -test, -test_label, -age_bin) |>
  knitr::kable()
sex_label age_label N N reversal 5yr N reversal 10yr N reversal 15yr N reversal 20yr

Women 55-59 334 46 30 15 6

Women 80-84 186 20 18 5 5

Men 55-59 299 47 7 4 0

# Save CSV (analysis) + formatted XLSX (delivery). write_table4_workbook() and
# make_output_name() are defined in workbook_writers.R / helpers.R.
write_csv(
  table4_exclusive,
  file.path(
    table_dir,
    make_output_name(
      paste0("xprize_pooled3_", model_tag, "_class2_table4_exclusive"),
      "csv"
    )
  )
)

table4_excl_xlsx <- file.path(
  table_dir,
  make_output_name(
    paste0("xprize_pooled3_", model_tag, "_class2_table4_exclusive"),
    "xlsx"
  )
)
write_table4_workbook(table4_exclusive, table4_excl_xlsx, form = "exclusive")

write_table4_workbook(
  table4_cumulative_pct, table4_cumul_pct_xlsx, form = "cumulative_pct"
)

4. Scatter plots

Table 4 gives only age-bin counts. These scatter plots show the participant-level data behind those counts, so we can see who is called a reverser and why.

Each point is one participant-test record:

  • x = baseline age. y = the direction-adjusted annualized observed change (positive = improvement, for every test, after the Trails sign-flip). This is the exact number the reversal rule compares to the model threshold.
  • The dashed line at y = 0 separates observed decline (below) from observed improvement (above).
  • Color = the maximum reversal horizon each record reached. Grey = none, and the color gets darker from 5y to 20y. A longer horizon means the point cleared a higher threshold, so it is a more extreme apparent reversal.
  • Panels are split by sex. The age axis is 50 to 90, to match Table 4.

Scatter plots display the participant-level observations underlying Table 4. The x-axis shows baseline age and the y-axis shows direction-adjusted annualized change. Points are colored by maximum qualifying reversal horizon and panels are separated by sex.

4.1 Check one test

# defined in spontaneous_reversal_functions.R
plot_reversal_scatter(reversal_subject_level, test_dictionary, "lmd")

4.2 Save all tests

scatter_order <- reversal_subject_level |>
  distinct(test) |>
  left_join(
    test_dictionary |> select(test, xprize_subdomain),
    by = "test"
  ) |>
  arrange(xprize_subdomain, test)

walk2(
  scatter_order$xprize_subdomain,
  scatter_order$test,
  function(subdomain_x, test_x) {

    p <- plot_reversal_scatter(
      reversal_subject_level, test_dictionary, test_x
    )

    subdomain_file <- subdomain_x |>
      str_to_lower() |>
      str_replace_all("&", "and") |>
      str_replace_all("[^a-z0-9]+", "_") |>
      str_replace_all("_+", "_") |>
      str_replace_all("^_|_$", "")

    test_file <- test_x |>
      str_to_lower() |>
      str_replace_all("[^a-z0-9]+", "_") |>
      str_replace_all("_+", "_") |>
      str_replace_all("^_|_$", "")

    ggsave(
      filename = file.path(
        plot_dir,
        paste0(
          "class2_reversal_scatter_",
          subdomain_file, "_", test_file,
          "_", output_suffix, ".pdf"
        )
      ),
      plot = p,
      width = 12,
      height = 6
    )
  }
)

cat("Scatter plots saved to:", plot_dir, "\n")
## Scatter plots saved to: ../output/plots/pooled3_05_spontaneous_reversal_class2_crude_2visit
